text: We started our meeting with my autumn reflections. Stating that autumn is the season where the seas prepare for the spring. And guess what? Next time we meet, it will be spring in London. And with that word of optimism, I wish you all a restful weekend and safe journey back home. Thank you very much. And it's not a surprise that I see the car of the United Kingdom. You know, the Kingdom we have the floor. Thank you, Chair. As is tradition as host government, we wanted to say a few departing words to all distinct Welsh delegates present and online. This has been a challenging time for many of us. However, from this week's rich and fruitful discussions, we do leave today with a renewed sense of optimism. We thank you, Chair, and your leadership steering us to more calm and gentle waters in the recent days. We would also like to thank the Secretary-at and all building, IT and catering staff for all of their tireless efforts. Without you, none of our work would be possible. Today is United Nations Day, marking 80 years since the ratification of the UN Charter in 1945. 80 years since the birth of multi-naptalism. The UK is very proud to host this organisation and its history of six months. It is our sincere hope that in 80 years' time future generations will look back and know that we, like those before us, rose to the challenges of the day together. In the meantime, on behalf of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, we wish all delegates a safe journey home and some well-earned rest. Whilst we cannot promise warm weather, we look forward to warmly welcoming you all back very soon. Thank you. Thank you, the United Kingdom and before you leave the room, there will be a meeting here next week, so you need to tidy up your stuff and so we need to act quickly out of air because they will need to set up the meeting for next week. So with that, I now close our meeting. Thank you very much, everybody. Thank you.
